definition: A simple shelter with a single sloping roof, typically built against a wall or larger structure.
example: They slept in a rough lean-to at the edge of the forest.

definition: Assuming power or authority without justification; arrogantly domineering.
example: She gave an imperious wave of her hand, expecting everyone to fall in line.

definition: Insisting on immediate compliance; admitting no contradiction or refusal.
example: The peremptory tone of the memo left no room for discussion.

definition: The highest point of development or achievement; the elevation of someone to divine status.
example: The late quartet is considered the apotheosis of Beethoven's compositional genius.

definition: Of or relating to fideism — the view that religious knowledge rests on faith rather than reason or evidence.
example: His fideistic stance held that some truths simply could not be reached by argument alone.

definition: Utterly bewildered or perplexed.
example: She was completely flummoxed by the contradictory instructions.

definition: Pale and appearing weak or ill; faintly colored.
example: He gave a wan smile and said he was fine, but no one believed him.

definition: Lacking something or feeling a keen sense of loss.
example: She was bereft of words when she heard the news.

definition: Deprived of land, property, or rights; stripped of what one had.
example: The dispossessed farmers had nowhere left to go.

definition: In an embarrassed, meek manner, as if aware of having done something wrong.
example: He sheepishly admitted he had forgotten her birthday.

definition: Skillful and quick in movement or handling; nimble.
example: With a few deft strokes, she sketched a convincing likeness.

definition: An uncouth, aggressive, or boorish person.
example: A group of louts had taken over the back corner of the pub.

definition: Glowing with intense heat; by extension, brilliantly animated or furiously angry.
example: The director was incandescent with rage when he saw the final cut.

definition: Stubbornly resistant to authority, instruction, or discipline.
example: The recalcitrant student refused to comply despite repeated warnings.

definition: Seemingly without end; tiresomely long.
example: The interminable meeting finally broke at half past seven.

definition: The quality of being pleasantly sharp or stimulating; provocative interest or charm.
example: The story gained its piquancy from what was left unsaid.

definition: To have a noticeable effect or impact, often negative; to encroach on.
example: The noise from the construction site began to impinge on her concentration.

definition: In a state of pitiful intellectual or moral ignorance.
example: He dismissed his critics as benighted provincials who had missed the point entirely.

definition: Overstepping proper limits; failing to observe appropriate boundaries of deference.
example: It would be presumptuous to assume the invitation extends to guests.

definition: To cite as evidence or bring forward as a point in an argument.
example: He adduced several historical examples to support his case.

definition: Extremely bad or unpleasant; utterly detestable.
example: The food was execrable — cold, tasteless, and poorly presented.

definition: Grimly mocking or cynical; expressing contempt through irony.
example: He raised an eyebrow in sardonic amusement at the suggestion.

definition: Behavior or mannerisms adopted to impress others; pretentious artificiality.
example: His use of obscure vocabulary was pure affectation — he wanted to sound learned.

definition: To treat someone as a child or reduce them to a childlike state.
example: Overly simplified explanations can infantilize the audience.

definition: Trite, unoriginal remarks intended to soothe or reassure; platitudes.
example: His speech was full of bromides about resilience and community.

definition: To remove a difficulty or obstacle in advance; to anticipate and prevent.
example: The new protocol was designed to obviate the need for manual checks.

definition: Lacking imagination or originality; dull and matter-of-fact.
example: The explanation was prosaic but accurate.

definition: Kept secret, especially because it would not be approved; done by stealth.
example: She cast a surreptitious glance at his notes during the exam.

definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way.
example: The pernicious effects of misinformation take years to undo.

definition: Shortsightedness; by extension, a lack of foresight or intellectual breadth.
example: The policy reflected a dangerous myopia about long-term consequences.

definition: In very poor condition through neglect; or, a person without home or occupation.
example: The derelict warehouse had been empty for thirty years.

definition: A strong preference or natural liking for something.
example: She had a predilection for 19th-century French novels.

definition: A claim to something; also, pretentious behavior or attitude.
example: He had no pretension to expertise — he was simply curious.

definition: Calm, confident, and in control of one's feelings and behavior.
example: Even under cross-examination, she remained entirely self-possessed.

definition: Denoting a period that was idyllically happy and peaceful.
example: He looked back on the halcyon days of his childhood with uncomplicated affection.

definition: Demonstrating aggression and willingness to fight; warlike.
example: The bellicose rhetoric from both sides made diplomacy feel impossible.

definition: Mutual indwelling or interpenetration; in theology, the coexistence of the persons of the Trinity within one another.
example: The doctrine of coinherence holds that the divine persons are inseparable without being identical.

definition: Using very few words; brief and terse in expression.
example: His reply was characteristically laconic: a single word.

definition: Tending to talk a great deal; garrulous.
example: The loquacious host filled every silence with another anecdote.

definition: Scrupulously clean; by extension, coldly precise and lacking warmth.
example: The office had an antiseptic quality — efficient, tidy, and impersonal.

definition: To tear something into pieces with force; to cause great distress.
example: The news rent the community in two.

definition: Successful in producing a desired result; effective.
example: The treatment proved efficacious in clinical trials.

definition: Physical or mental weakness; a particular illness or ailment.
example: He bore his infirmity with quiet dignity.

definition: Not conspicuous or attracting attention; inconspicuous.
example: The camera mount was small and unobtrusive.

definition: To remain in existence; to continue permanently.
example: The question is whether any personal identity perdures through radical change.

definition: A secondary effect or byproduct that arises from a process but does not causally influence it.
example: Some argue that consciousness is an epiphenomenon of neural computation.

definition: Feeling or expressing overwhelming joy or rapture.
example: The crowd was ecstatic when the final whistle blew.

definition: Not having any serious purpose or value; carefree and lighthearted.
example: The judge dismissed the lawsuit as frivolous.

definition: Characterized by severe self-discipline and abstention from pleasure; a person who practices such discipline.
example: He lived an ascetic life — no television, sparse meals, cold showers.

definition: A legacy; a thing left to someone in a will.
example: She received a generous bequest from her aunt's estate.

definition: Tending to obstruct or harm; unfriendly; hostile.
example: The conditions were inimical to sustained creative work.

definition: Aggressively greedy or grasping; voracious.
example: The rapacious landlord raised rents every few months.

definition: Lacking a plan, purpose, or connection; occurring randomly or fitfully.
example: They made desultory conversation while waiting for the train.

definition: To reveal the presence of; to demonstrate clearly.
example: Her work evinces a rare command of both the technical and the expressive.

definition: Showing a lack of reverence for something regarded as sacred; irreverent.
example: The satirist's impious humor provoked outrage in some quarters.

definition: To mark with spots or rounded patches of color or shade.
example: Sunlight dappled the forest floor through the canopy.

definition: To take or claim something for oneself without justification.
example: The committee arrogated powers that properly belonged to the full assembly.

definition: Having the same boundaries or extent; coinciding exactly in scope.
example: The diocese was roughly coterminous with the old county boundaries.

definition: To charm or captivate, sometimes deceptively; to pass time pleasantly.
example: He was beguiled by her apparent openness, only to realize it was a performance.

definition: The place of origin or earliest known history; the documented record of ownership.
example: The painting's provenance was murky — several owners were unaccounted for.

definition: Highly pertinent or appropriate to a particular situation.
example: Her citation was apposite — the passage said exactly what the argument required.

definition: Skillful use of the hands in performing tricks; deception or trickery.
example: The accounting involved some financial legerdemain that kept the auditors busy.

definition: With great care, persistence, and diligence.
example: She had assiduously avoided taking sides in the dispute.

definition: To agree or express agreement; agreement or approval.
example: He gave a slow nod of assent.

definition: A person who makes a claim, especially to a title, right, or benefit.
example: Several claimants disputed the inheritance.

definition: The particular sense or meaning in which a word or phrase is understood.
example: In its common acceptation, the word carries a slightly pejorative flavor.

definition: In relation to; compared with; face to face with.
example: The question is where we stand vis-à-vis our competitors.

definition: A container or space for holding something.
example: The old tin served as a receptacle for loose change.

definition: Describing God or the divine through positive affirmations of what God is.
example: Cataphatic theology speaks of God as loving, powerful, and present.

definition: Describing God or the divine by negation — saying what God is not rather than what God is.
example: Apophatic theology holds that God transcends all human categories.

definition: Fame and respect within a field; a position of superiority or prominence.
example: She achieved eminence in the field relatively early in her career.

definition: Having clearly determined or fixed limits or boundaries.
example: The project scope was carefully delimited to avoid scope creep.

definition: A thing that existed before or logically precedes another.
example: We can identify the historical antecedents of the current crisis.

definition: The quality of being inherent or pervasively present within the world; remaining within rather than transcending.
example: The doctrine of divine immanence holds that God is present within creation.

definition: To irritate or annoy someone.
example: His dismissive tone nettled her more than she let on.

definition: To have a positive or negative effect on; to come back upon.
example: Her success redounded to the credit of the whole department.

definition: The act of making amends for guilt or wrongdoing; atonement.
example: He spent years in quiet expiation for the harm he had caused.

definition: Loud and powerful in sound.
example: The professor's stentorian voice carried easily to the back of the hall.

definition: To be a powerful factor working against something.
example: The lack of infrastructure militated against rapid economic development.

definition: A turning point marking a critical division or change in direction.
example: The verdict was a watershed moment for civil rights in the country.

definition: Wickedness and depravity, especially as revealed in criminal or immoral conduct.
example: The charges included moral turpitude.

definition: Optimistic or positive, especially in a difficult situation.
example: He remained sanguine about the outcome despite the setbacks.

definition: To remedy or set right a wrong; compensation or reparation for a wrong.
example: They sought legal redress for the losses they had suffered.

definition: In a way that is impossible to avoid or escape; inevitably.
example: The argument ineluctably leads to a contradiction.

definition: Care and concern for the well-being of someone; anxious consideration.
example: She inquired about his health with genuine solicitude.

definition: To feel or express great loathing for; to curse or denounce vehemently.
example: The press execrated the decision as a betrayal of public trust.

definition: Abrupt or offhand in manner; bluntly curt.
example: His brusque reply left her unsure whether she had offended him.

definition: The phase of the heartbeat when the heart muscle contracts and pumps blood.
example: Blood pressure is measured at both systole and diastole.

definition: Giving or indicating a good chance of success; favorable.
example: It was not a propitious moment to raise the question.

definition: Rude and disrespectful behavior; impudent disregard for others.
example: The insolence of his reply left the entire committee speechless.

definition: Cloudy or opaque with suspended matter; by extension, confused or muddled.
example: The river ran turbid after the storm.

definition: Deserving or causing public disgrace or shame.
example: The team suffered an ignominious defeat in the first round.

definition: To drink plentifully and be merry; to engage in boisterous celebration.
example: The sailors spent their shore leave carousing in the port town.

definition: A claim to something that is false; a false show of purpose or appearance.
example: He arrived under the pretense of returning her book.

definition: The sky or heavens; figuratively, a particular sphere of activity.
example: She was a rising star in the literary firmament.

definition: Habitual or natural combinations of words; the tendency of words to co-occur.
example: Strong tea, not powerful tea — learning a language means learning its collocations.

definition: Overcome with anger; relating to or denoting apoplexy (stroke).
example: The editor was apoplectic when she saw the front page.

definition: Intelligently analytical and clear-thinking; sharp and direct.
example: Her incisive questions cut through the evasion immediately.

definition: A departure from what is normal or expected; an anomaly.
example: The error was dismissed as an aberration rather than a systemic problem.

definition: Recklessly extravagant; having or showing a complete lack of moral principles.
example: The profligate spending of the previous administration had exhausted the reserves.

definition: To make false and harmful statements about someone; to slander.
example: Political opponents had calumniated him for years without evidence.

definition: Showy but superficially attractive decoration; something gaudy and of little value.
example: Beneath the tinsel of the ceremony was a hollow ritual.

definition: An unrestrained outpouring of feeling; the seeping out of a fluid.
example: He acknowledged the compliment without responding in kind to her effusion.

definition: Given to moralizing in a pompous or self-righteous way; full of pithy maxims.
example: The editorial was sententious without being particularly insightful.

definition: Relating to the sensual, spontaneous, and emotionally uninhibited; after Dionysus, god of wine.
example: The festival had a Dionysian energy that made careful people nervous.

definition: An area of muddy or boggy ground; a complex and confusing situation.
example: The project descended into a morass of competing priorities.

definition: A form of government or political organization; a politically organized community.
example: The Greek city-state was the original model of a self-governing polity.

definition: Childishly sulky or bad-tempered; unreasonably irritable.
example: His petulant response to criticism suggested he had never faced real scrutiny before.

definition: Suave, courteous, and refined in manner; polished.
example: He was unfailingly urbane, even when delivering disagreeable news.

definition: Humor or lightness, especially when applied to a serious matter.
example: A moment of levity broke the tension in the room.

definition: The religious act of anointing with oil; by extension, exaggerated or insincere earnestness of manner.
example: He spoke with an unction that made everything sound more solemn than it was.

definition: Just begun and not fully formed; undeveloped; rudimentary.
example: The idea was still inchoate — suggestive but without clear shape.

definition: Annoyingly insensitive or slow to understand; (of an angle) greater than 90 degrees.
example: I wasn't sure if he was being obtuse on purpose.

definition: Settled comfortably and firmly in a position or place.
example: She was ensconced in the armchair by the window with a book.

definition: Attractive or appealing in appearance or manner; making a favorable first impression.
example: He was not particularly prepossessing, but his ideas were compelling.

definition: Relentlessly severe, stern, or gloomy in manner or appearance.
example: A dour man of few words, he rarely smiled.

definition: Tending to find fault or raise objections on petty grounds; naggingly critical.
example: The captious reviewer picked at the footnotes while ignoring the argument.

definition: Having or showing keen mental discernment and good judgment; wise.
example: Her sagacious advice had helped him through several professional crises.

definition: Fluent and easy in speech but lacking depth or sincerity; superficial.
example: His glib reassurances did nothing to address the underlying concern.

definition: A custom, phrase, or use of language that identifies membership in a group; a long-standing belief or practice that distinguishes insiders from outsiders.
example: Citing that particular theorist had become a shibboleth among the department's true believers.

definition: Made to a particular specification; custom-made; tailored.
example: He wore a bespoke suit and carried himself accordingly.

definition: To take in and absorb fully; to become part of a larger group or culture.
example: It takes time to assimilate new information into an existing framework.

definition: Inability to take effective action; helplessness.
example: She felt a deep sense of impotence in the face of bureaucratic obstruction.

definition: To exist as a permanent or essential quality of something; to be inherent.
example: The tension inheres in the very structure of the argument.

definition: Reserved, modest, and shy in manner or behavior.
example: She gave a demure smile and looked away.

definition: Resembling a palace; spacious, grand, and splendid.
example: By the standards of the neighborhood, the apartment was palatial.

definition: Waste or debris; remains from disintegration or erosion.
example: The street was littered with the detritus of last night's festival.

definition: Capable of grasping; (of a body part) adapted for seizing or holding.
example: Spider monkeys use their prehensile tails as a fifth limb.

definition: Having or showing a feeling of vague longing or regret for something past.
example: She looked out the window with a wistful expression.

definition: Relating to or characteristic of boxing or physical fighting.
example: His pugilistic posture made clear that the conversation was not going to be collegial.

definition: To strip bare; to remove all covering from.
example: Decades of logging had denuded the hillside.

definition: Having lost vitality, strength, or energy; weakened.
example: The heat left her enervated and unable to concentrate.

definition: Ready insight into and understanding of things; shrewdness.
example: With characteristic perspicacity, she identified the flaw in the argument immediately.

definition: Not able to be corrected, reformed, or improved; habitually bad.
example: He was an incorrigible optimist, immune to discouragement.

definition: Looking exhausted and unwell, especially from prolonged hardship or worry.
example: He returned from the expedition looking haggard and ten years older.

definition: An image or representation of something; an unsatisfactory imitation or substitute.
example: What remained was merely a simulacrum of the original community.

definition: Accusations made in response to accusations; mutual blame.
example: The meeting dissolved into recriminations and no progress was made.

definition: Liable to change; easily altered; (in psychology) emotionally unstable.
example: The patient presented with labile mood — cycling rapidly between states.

definition: In a quiet voice so as not to be overheard; under one's breath.
example: He remarked sotto voce that the whole exercise was pointless.

definition: Less distinguished followers or imitators of a great writer, artist, or thinker.
example: His school produced a generation of epigones who repeated his ideas without advancing them.

definition: To uproot from a natural or traditional environment; to destroy the cultural or social roots of.
example: Rapid urbanization deracinated communities that had been stable for generations.

definition: To transform, especially in a surprising or magical manner.
example: The old factory had been transmogrified into a luxury apartment complex.

definition: A person who has or claims to have a lot of authority or influence.
example: Every minor panjandrum in the organization wanted to be consulted before any decision was made.

definition: Using an unnecessarily large number of words to express an idea; indirect and circumlocutory.
example: His writing style was periphrastic to a fault — he never said in one word what he could say in ten.

definition: Lacking in vitality, force, or effectiveness; pale and weak.
example: The reform was anemic — too timid to change anything of substance.

definition: Not likely to cause offense or disagreement; pain-relieving; something that soothes.
example: The statement was anodyne to the point of meaninglessness.

definition: Difficult to understand; obscure.
example: The paper was abstruse even by the standards of the field.

definition: Excessively interested in acquiring money or material things; greedy.
example: An acquisitive culture tends to measure success in units of accumulation.

definition: The act of treating an abstraction as if it were a concrete, real thing.
example: Calling it a 'structure' risks reification — the pattern may be a statistical artifact.

definition: Reversion to an earlier ancestral type; reappearance of a characteristic after a long absence.
example: His politics seemed like an atavism — ideas from a century ago, reasserting themselves.

definition: The scientific study of measurements and proportions of the human body.
example: Early criminologists misused anthropometry to make claims about criminal types.

definition: A cowshed.
example: The smell from the byre drifted across the yard each morning.

definition: Searching out and publicly exposing wrongdoing by prominent individuals or institutions.
example: The magazine built its reputation on muckraking journalism.

definition: The views associated with psychologist Arthur Jensen, particularly the hereditarian hypothesis regarding group differences in IQ.
example: Jensenism became a flashpoint for debates about race, genetics, and scientific responsibility.

definition: By the grace of God (Latin); a phrase traditionally used in royal titles.
example: The coin bore the inscription: 'Elizabeth II dei gratia regina.'

definition: Clever or skillful in using one's hands or mind.
example: She was adroit at steering conversations away from difficult territory.

definition: Wary and cautious; unwilling to take risks; considering all circumstances carefully.
example: He was circumspect in his public statements, knowing how easily words were misread.

definition: Mental calmness and composure, especially in a difficult situation.
example: She received the diagnosis with remarkable equanimity.

definition: Sad and disappointed; dejected.
example: He returned crestfallen, his plan having been rejected without discussion.

definition: Not pretentious or arrogant; modest and unpretentious.
example: Despite her accomplishments, she was unassuming and easy to talk to.

definition: Standing out so as to be clearly visible; attracting notice or attention.
example: His absence from the ceremony was conspicuous.

definition: Making or constituting a harsh or loud noise; boisterously loud.
example: A raucous cheer went up from the crowd.

definition: To make petty or unnecessary objections; a trivial objection.
example: It would be churlish to cavil at such a generous offer.

definition: To avoid committing to a decision in order to gain time; to act evasively.
example: He temporized for weeks before finally giving an answer.

definition: Showing a lack of courage or determination; timid and faint-hearted.
example: The pusillanimous response from leadership emboldened the opposition.

definition: Decisively settling a legal case or question; relating to or bringing about a resolution.
example: The court found the precedent dispositive and ruled accordingly.

definition: The process of logical reasoning; forming judgments through careful argument.
example: His ratiocination was impressive but led to a conclusion that common sense rejected.

definition: Not telling the truth; lying.
example: The report was mendacious on several key points.

definition: A person who cultivates an interest in a subject without serious commitment; an amateur dabbler.
example: He was dismissed as a dilettante by the professionals, but his intuitions proved sharper.

definition: To walk or move wearily or reluctantly over a distance.
example: She had traipsed across the city in the rain looking for the venue.

definition: Sleepy and drowsy; tending to induce sleep.
example: The somnolent afternoon heat made concentration nearly impossible.

definition: Pale, especially in a way that suggests poor health or weakness; lacking vitality.
example: She looked pallid and drawn after a week of poor sleep.

definition: In keeping with good taste and propriety; polite and restrained.
example: The gathering was decorous — no one raised their voice.

definition: Shedding leaves annually; by extension, not permanent.
example: The deciduous trees along the road were bare by November.

definition: Suffering from indigestion; by extension, irritable and bad-tempered.
example: His dyspeptic commentary on modern culture had its own grim appeal.

definition: Modest or shy due to a lack of self-confidence.
example: Her diffident manner concealed a considerable intelligence.

definition: To deliberately avoid or abstain from something.
example: He eschewed publicity and let his work speak for itself.

definition: Bitter and abusive in language; full of harsh criticism.
example: The vituperative review was more revealing about the critic than the book.

definition: The belief that moral laws are not binding, especially when superseded by divine grace or faith.
example: The heresy commission charged him with antinomianism — he held that the saved were exempt from law.

definition: So surprised and confused as to be unsure how to react.
example: She was nonplussed by the question and took a long moment to respond.

definition: A person who makes a humble request or prayer to someone with power.
example: He arrived not as a partner but as a supplicant, hat in hand.

definition: To outline or indicate faintly; to foreshadow.
example: The introduction adumbrates the argument without fully developing it.

definition: A formal or high-flown expression of praise.
example: The retirement dinner produced encomium after encomium, each more extravagant than the last.

definition: Growing old; in the process of aging or deterioration.
example: The senescent institution clung to practices that had outlived their purpose.

definition: A word for the inhabitants of a particular place, derived from the place name.
example: The demonym for someone from Glasgow is Glaswegian.

definition: Making scandalous, abusive, or grossly unfair claims; given to coarse language.
example: The pamphlet was scurrilous and had no basis in fact.

definition: Unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable.
example: There was an ineluctable logic to the situation — they had no real choice.

definition: A person who refuses to conform to an established authority or practice; historically, a Catholic who refused to attend Church of England services.
example: He was the department's lone recusant, voting against every consensus position.

definition: Attempting to avoid notice; secretive and sly.
example: He cast a furtive glance over his shoulder before entering.

definition: Producing good effects; beneficial, especially through being corrective.
example: The failure had a salutary effect — it forced a long-overdue reassessment.

definition: A person who is between 70 and 79 years old.
example: The committee was dominated by septuagenarians with no memory of why the rules existed.

definition: Tending or able to change frequently or easily; versatile; taking many forms.
example: The concept is protean — it means something different in every discipline that uses it.

definition: Used without an article; in grammar, lacking the definite or indefinite article.
example: The anarthrous use of 'God' in that verse has been debated by translators for centuries.

definition: A state of temporary suspension or inactivity.
example: The project was held in abeyance pending the outcome of the review.

definition: The state of being doubtful; uncertainty.
example: She expressed her dubiety about the timeline without openly objecting.

definition: To be a sign or warning of something, especially something momentous or ominous.
example: No one knew what the unusual silence portended.

definition: A direct vote by all citizens on an important question of policy.
example: The question of independence was put to the population in a plebiscite.

definition: Inciting or causing people to rebel against the authority of a state.
example: The pamphlet was seized as seditious material.

definition: Having developed certain abilities at an earlier age than usual.
example: The precocious child was reading novels before she started school.

definition: A line of people or vehicles preventing access to an area; to form such a barrier.
example: Police established a cordon around the building.

definition: Used in brackets after a quoted word to indicate that it is reproduced exactly as it appears in the original, despite apparent errors.
example: The letter read: 'We are very exited [sic] to announce the merger.'

definition: A name or nickname.
example: He went by the moniker 'The Professor' long after he'd left academia.

definition: Not friendly or forthcoming; keeping a cool distance.
example: He remained aloof throughout the reception, speaking only when spoken to.

definition: An excessive amount of something; to cause disgust through overindulgence.
example: A surfeit of information can be as paralyzing as a shortage.

definition: Silly or foolish; mildly crazy.
example: It was a daft idea, but everyone went along with it.

definition: To speak or act evasively; to avoid stating the truth directly.
example: He prevaricated for so long that the question answered itself.

definition: Severe or strict in manner; having no comforts or luxuries; plain and unadorned.
example: The room was austere — white walls, a single desk, nothing unnecessary.

definition: Giving or suggesting a promising start; favorable.
example: It was not an auspicious beginning to the expedition.

definition: Filled or well-supplied with something; sated with food.
example: The text is replete with examples, not all of them well-chosen.

definition: Of or relating to intense pain along a nerve; by extension, acutely sensitive or painful.
example: The question of funding had become neuralgic — everyone winced at the mention of it.

definition: To jump or dance around excitedly; to engage in lively or boisterous activity.
example: The children cavorted in the spray from the garden hose.

definition: Of or like a portent; giving a sign of things to come; pompously weighty.
example: His portentous tone made the announcement feel more significant than it was.

definition: Coming before; anticipatory; in theology, describing grace that precedes and enables human response.
example: The doctrine of prevenient grace holds that God acts before any human initiative.

definition: Offense or annoyance, typically at a perceived slight.
example: She took umbrage at the suggestion that her work needed supervision.

definition: An extremely confused, complicated, or embarrassing situation.
example: The diplomatic imbroglio took months to untangle.

definition: Excessive growth or development of an organ or part; by extension, any overdevelopment.
example: The bureaucracy had undergone a hypertrophy that made it nearly unmanageable.

definition: Polite and well-bred, sometimes affectedly so; associated with respectability.
example: The neighborhood had a genteel shabbiness — once grand, now quietly declining.

definition: A person who is hostile or indifferent to culture and the arts; smugly conventional.
example: He called anyone who preferred popular music a philistine.

definition: Having or showing a tendency to be easily angered.
example: The irascible dean had reduced more than one graduate student to tears.

definition: A false, absurd, or distorted representation of something; a debased imitation.
example: The proceedings were a travesty — the verdict had been decided in advance.

definition: Impossible to solve or explain; unable to be dissolved.
example: The conflict seemed insoluble as long as both sides refused to negotiate.

definition: Beginning to happen or develop; in an early stage.
example: He caught the incipient problem before it became a crisis.

definition: Hypocritical and sanctimonious talk; language specific to a group; or, to tilt at an angle.
example: The speech was full of political cant that said nothing.

definition: Loud and clear; a shrill trumpet, or a strongly expressed call to action.
example: The report was a clarion call for reform.

definition: Noisy arguments or protests; a disturbance or uproar.
example: There would be ructions in the department if the proposal went through.

definition: The use of tricks or deception to achieve one's purpose.
example: The deal was riddled with chicanery — small print that reversed the apparent terms.

definition: A person who is new to a subject, skill, or belief; a novice.
example: Even a neophyte could see that the approach was fundamentally flawed.

definition: Relating to a disease that can be transmitted from animals to humans.
example: The zoonotic origins of the outbreak were traced to a wet market.

definition: A project that wastes time and money while appearing worthwhile; unnecessary work.
example: Critics dismissed the infrastructure project as a boondoggle.

definition: An extensive and destructive fire; a large-scale conflict.
example: A local dispute that no one expected to spread became a regional conflagration.

definition: Expressing a particular point of view; calculated to promote a cause.
example: The documentary was tendentious — every fact selected to support one conclusion.

definition: A state of ill humor or disorder; a type of paint; a viral disease in animals.
example: He was in a distemper all morning, snapping at anyone who approached.

definition: Highly ornate and complex; relating to the European artistic style of the 17th century.
example: The argument was baroque in its complexity — beautiful but hard to follow.

definition: Extending over the same space or time; having the same scope.
example: The jurisdiction of the court was coextensive with the territory of the state.

definition: The art of investigating truth through reasoned argument; the tension between opposing ideas that drives toward resolution.
example: The dialectic between freedom and equality structures much of political philosophy.

definition: To change the natural properties of something; to make unfit for consumption; to cause a protein to lose its functional shape.
example: Heat denatures the protein, causing it to unfold and lose its activity.

definition: Chiefly encountered in éminence grise: a person who exercises power or influence without holding an official position.
example: He had no title but was the department's éminence grise — nothing happened without his approval.

definition: To pass out of sight or existence; to fade away gradually.
example: The sense of possibility evanesced as the years passed.

definition: To conceal or disguise one's true feelings or intentions.
example: She dissimulated her disappointment well, congratulating him warmly.

definition: A change of circumstances, especially an unwelcome one; the ups and downs of life.
example: He had survived the vicissitudes of a long career with his reputation intact.

definition: Moving from topic to topic without strict logic; digressive; also, relating to discourse.
example: His lectures were discursive but always arrived somewhere worth reaching.

definition: Causing harm or evil; actively malicious.
example: The policy had maleficent consequences that its designers had not anticipated.
